title: Research presentation — structure and delivery
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to structuring and delivering the IB Theatre HL Research Presentation. It deconstructs the assessment criteria, offering strategies for synthesising research and praxis, crafting a compelling narrative structure, and delivering a presentation that meets the top markband descriptors.
learningObjectives: Analyse the assessment criteria for the Research Presentation (A, B, C, D) to understand examiner expectations.
Develop a clear, coherent, and engaging structure for the presentation that seamlessly integrates research and practical exploration.
Select and justify a 'moment of theatre' that effectively demonstrates the application of theory to practice (praxis).
Refine presentation and delivery skills to communicate complex ideas with clarity, confidence, and theatrical engagement.
Understand how to demonstrate synthesis by articulating the connections between research, practical exploration, and personal learning.

simpleExplanation.title: The Theatre Detective's Report
simpleExplanation.summary: The Research Presentation is not just a report on a theatre tradition; it's you, as a theatre-maker, showing how you've used that research to create something. You are demonstrating the vital link between academic knowledge and embodied practice.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you're a detective investigating a classic case (a world theatre tradition). First, you gather all the evidence from archives and witness statements (Criterion A: Research). Then, you re-enact a key moment of the case yourself to understand the perpetrator's methods (Criterion B: Praxis). Finally, you present your findings to the chief, not by reading a dry report, but by showing them the re-enactment and explaining how it unlocks the entire case (Criteria C & D: Presentation & Synthesis). Your presentation is the story of your investigation.
steps: Frame Your Inquiry: Select a specific, researchable world theatre tradition and formulate a focused research question that fascinates you as a performer or director.
Synthesise Research & Praxis: Conduct academic research while simultaneously exploring the tradition's conventions physically. Let your practical work inform your research questions, and vice-versa.
Craft the Narrative & 'Moment': Structure your presentation as a compelling story. Select and refine a powerful 'moment of theatre' that serves as the climax of your practical demonstration.

Deliver and Justify: Present your findings and your 'moment' to an audience, clearly articulating the 'how' and 'why' of your choices and demonstrating the synthesis of your theoretical and practical learning.

flashcards.back: The process of putting theoretical knowledge into practice. In the RP, it's the application of your research on a convention into a 'moment of theatre'. It is 'thinking through doing'.
A specific practice, technique, or method that is characteristic of a particular theatre tradition or practitioner (e.g., the 'neutral mask' in Lecoq, the 'Jo-ha-kyū' structure in Noh theatre).
The practical demonstration component of the RP. It is a short, focused piece of theatre that applies a convention from your chosen tradition to communicate meaning.

Connecting and integrating different elements to form a new understanding. In the RP, it means demonstrating the relationship between your research, your practical exploration, the moment of theatre, and your own learning as a theatre-maker.
Explaining the reasoning behind your choices. You must justify why you chose a particular convention, how you applied it, and what effect it had in your moment of theatre.
A research method where the primary evidence is generated from the researcher's own physical, practical experience. Your practical explorations are a form of embodied research.

The theory and practice of dramatic composition and theatrical representation. In the context of the RP, it refers to the intentional structuring of your presentation to create a specific journey for the audience.
Assesses the depth, breadth, and focus of your research into the chosen world theatre tradition and its cultural context. Top marks require specific, well-cited, and relevant research.

Assesses your practical exploration and application of the tradition's conventions, culminating in the moment of theatre. Top marks require a clear and effective application of conventions to create meaning.
Assesses the structure of your presentation and your delivery skills. Top marks require a coherent, engaging structure and a dynamic, clear delivery that demonstrates theatrical awareness.
Verbal cues used during a presentation to help the audience understand the structure and follow the argument. E.g., 'I will now move on to discuss how this research informed my practical choices...'
The ability to produce a desired or intended result. When reflecting, consider the efficacy of your choices: how effective was your application of the convention in achieving your artistic intention?

faq.q: How 'polished' does my moment of theatre need to be?
faq.a: It does not need to be a perfect, professional-level performance. The focus is on the effective application of a convention to create meaning. The examiners are assessing your understanding and application (Criterion B), not your acting talent alone. A simple, clear, and well-justified demonstration is better than a complex, messy one.

faq.q: Can I use a PowerPoint or other visual aids?
faq.a: Yes, you can, but use them wisely. They fall under Criterion C (Presentation). They should support your presentation, not become it. Use high-quality images, short video clips (if relevant and brief), or key terms. Avoid slides full of text that you just read aloud. You should be the focus.

faq.q: What is the difference between 'synthesis' and just describing what I did?
faq.a: Description is 'I researched Stanislavski's 'magic if'. Then I performed a scene where I asked 'what if I were this character?'.' Synthesis is 'My research into Stanislavski's 'magic if' revealed its purpose was to stimulate the actor's imagination to create truthful behaviour. By applying this, I discovered that asking 'what if' allowed me to access a personal emotional connection to the character's dilemma, which resulted in a more authentic and less indicated performance, demonstrating the direct link between his psychological theory and practical, believable acting.'

faq.q: How many sources do I need for Criterion A?
faq.a: There is no magic number. The key is quality and relevance, not quantity. A top-scoring presentation will typically use 3-5 high-quality, specific sources (e.g., academic books, journal articles, practitioner interviews) and integrate them meaningfully, rather than just listing ten websites. Show that you have engaged deeply with a few key texts.

faq.q: Can my 'moment of theatre' be a directorial presentation?
faq.a: Yes. You can direct another student or group of students in your moment of theatre. In this case, your presentation would focus on your directorial choices and how you instructed the performers to apply the convention. Your justification would be about your directorial process and its efficacy.

sections.content: The Research Presentation (RP) is a cornerstone of the HL Theatre course, uniquely blending academic inquiry with practical, embodied performance. It challenges you to become a 'researcher-practitioner', moving beyond simply reporting on a theatre tradition to actively using it. This lesson will equip you with the tools to structure a sophisticated presentation and deliver it with theatrical flair, targeting the highest markbands.
Deconstructing the Assessment Criteria: What Examiners Reward
Understanding the criteria is the first step to success. Examiners use them as a precise checklist. Let's translate them into actionable goals.

items: **Criterion A: The Researcher (Max 6 marks)** - Go beyond Wikipedia. Use academic journals, primary sources (practitioner writings), and interviews. Show you understand not just *what* the convention is, but *why* it exists within its cultural and historical context. A top-band presentation cites specific sources and details.

**Criterion B: The Practitioner (Max 8 marks)** - This is about your 'praxis'. You must clearly identify a convention, explore it physically, and then apply it to create meaning in your 'moment of theatre'. The link between the convention and your intention must be explicit and effective. Examiners look for thoughtful application, not just imitation.

**Criterion C: The Presenter (Max 8 marks)** - Structure is key. A simple 'intro-body-conclusion' is not enough. Think theatrically: how can you create a compelling journey for your audience? Your physical and vocal delivery must be engaging and confident. This is a performance about performance.

**Criterion D: The Synthesiser (Max 8 marks)** - This is the highest-level skill. Synthesis is the 'so what?'. You must constantly connect the dots for the examiner: 'Because my research showed X, I explored it practically by doing Y, which resulted in Z in my moment of theatre, revealing a new understanding for me as a theatre-maker.' Avoid just listing facts or describing actions.

sections.question: Model Script Excerpt: A student is presenting on the use of 'Tension States' from Jacques Lecoq's pedagogy to explore the theme of societal pressure. This excerpt demonstrates synthesis (Criterion D) and justification of praxis (Criterion B).
sections.solution:
*(The student has just explained Lecoq's seven tension states, citing his book 'The Moving Body'.)*

"My research indicated that Lecoq developed these states not as emotional caricatures, but as muscular realities that reveal a character's relationship with their environment. I was particularly interested in the transition from 'Calm' (State 2) to 'Tense' (State 4) as a way to embody the creeping anxiety of external expectations. 

In my practical exploration, I discovered that simply 'acting' tense was ineffective. The key, as Lecoq suggests, was in the breath and the specific muscular engagement. I focused on restricting my diaphragm and raising the tension in my shoulders and neck. This physical choice had a direct impact on my psychological state, creating a genuine feeling of being trapped. 

This is the principle I applied in my moment of theatre. You will see my character begin in a state of relaxed, personal calm, but as invisible pressures mount, I will physically transition to State 4. The efficacy of this choice is that the internal struggle is made visible not through facial expression, but through a tangible, muscular transformation of the entire body, which I believe is the core of Lecoq's philosophy."

sections.content: Your presentation's structure is its dramaturgy. It should guide the audience, build suspense, and make your argument inescapable. Think of it as a well-directed play, not a lecture. The 'moment of theatre' should not be an awkward interruption; it should be the climax or a pivotal turning point in your narrative.

items: **The Hook:** Start with a question, a powerful image, or a brief physical demonstration to grab attention. Don't just say 'My presentation is on...'
**The Roadmap:** Clearly state your research question and signpost the main sections of your presentation. Let the audience know the journey you are taking them on.

**Integrated 'Moment':** Frame your 'moment of theatre' carefully. Introduce the context and your artistic intention before you perform it ('What you are about to see is...'). Afterwards, deconstruct it: 'As you saw, my choice to... was intended to...'. This frames the praxis within your argument.

**The 'Aha!' Moment (Synthesis):** Your conclusion should do more than summarise. It should articulate your key insight – the new understanding you gained by bridging the research and the practice. What did you learn about the tradition, about theatre, and about yourself as a creator?

sections.content: Record yourself rehearsing your presentation. Watch it back and assess it against Criterion C. Is your structure clear? Is your delivery engaging? Are you relying too much on notes? Self-assessment is crucial for refining your performance.
The 'Moment of Theatre': From Exploration to Efficacy

The 'moment of theatre' is the heart of your praxis (Criterion B). It is not about creating a polished, perfect performance. It is about demonstrating a deep, practical understanding of a convention. The process of getting there is as important as the final product.

items: **Focus on ONE Convention:** Do not try to showcase everything you learned. Choose one specific, tangible convention and explore it in depth. Quality over quantity.
**Document Your Process:** Keep a journal or blog. Note what worked, what didn't, and why. These reflections are gold for your presentation script, showing the journey of your practical exploration.
**Intention is Everything:** Be crystal clear about what you want to communicate in your moment. Your justification should link your application of the convention directly to this artistic intention.

**Frame it for the Audience:** The 'moment' will likely be abstract or decontextualized. It's your job to provide the context before and the analysis after, so the audience understands its significance within your research.

sections.question: Model Structure Outline: A student is creating a presentation on how the convention of 'Ma' (meaningful pause) in Japanese Noh theatre can be used to create dramatic tension in a contemporary monologue.
sections.solution:
**Presentation Title:** The Sound of Silence: Using Noh's 'Ma' to Amplify Modern Tension

1.  **Introduction (The Hook):** Begin by performing a single line from the monologue, followed by a long, charged 10-second pause ('Ma'). Then ask the audience: 'What did you feel in that silence?'.
2.  **The Inquiry (Roadmap):** State the research question: 'How can the Japanese Noh convention of 'Ma' be effectively applied to a contemporary Western monologue to heighten psychological tension?'. Briefly introduce Noh theatre and the concept of 'Ma', citing a key source like Kunio Komparu.
3.  **Research & Context:** Explain the cultural and philosophical underpinnings of 'Ma' in Noh. It's not just a pause; it's an energy-filled space/time. Discuss its relationship with 'Jo-ha-kyū'.
4.  **Praxis & Exploration (Bridge to the Moment):** Explain the practical experiments. 'I experimented with pauses of different lengths... I discovered that the physical stillness *during* the 'Ma' was crucial...'.
5.  **The Moment of Theatre:** 'I will now perform a one-minute excerpt from the monologue, applying my understanding of 'Ma'. Look for how the silences are used not to break the tension, but to build it.' (Performs the moment).
6.  **Deconstruction & Synthesis:** 'As you saw, the longest 'Ma' was placed just before the character's confession. This choice, directly inspired by Zeami Motokiyo's writings on creating anticipation, forces the audience to lean in and makes the subsequent revelation more impactful. My research into theory was made tangible through this practical application.'
7.  **Conclusion (The 'Aha!'):** Conclude that 'Ma' is not an absence of action, but a potent theatrical action in itself. Reflect on how this has changed your approach to rhythm and pacing as a performer.

sections.content: Your delivery is assessed under Criterion C. You are a theatre student; your presentation should reflect that. This means being conscious of your use of voice, body, and space to engage your audience and communicate your ideas with clarity and passion.

items: **Own the Space:** Don't hide behind a lectern. Move with purpose. Use gesture to emphasise points. Make eye contact with your audience (including the camera).
**Vocal Dynamics:** Vary your pace, pitch, and volume. Use pauses for emphasis. Your voice is a tool to maintain interest and convey meaning.

**Notes are a Tool, Not a Script:** Use cue cards with key points, not a full script to be read. Reading directly to the audience disengages them and demonstrates a lack of confidence and ownership of the material.

**Technology with Purpose:** If you use slides (e.g., PowerPoint, Google Slides), they should be minimal and visual. Use images, key quotes, or single words. Do not put your entire script on the screen. You are the presentation, not the slides.

sections.label: Test your knowledge of the Research Presentation
